1: I revealed myself to those who did not ask for me; I was found by those who did not seek me. To a nation that did not call on my name I said: Here am I, here am I! The divine self-disclosure to the non-seeking is the Gentile-mission oracle. Paul quotes this verse directly in Romans 10:20 and says Isaiah boldly declares what happened through his own apostolic mission: the gospel went to those who had not been looking for YHWH — the Gentiles. The here-am-I-here-am-I is the insistent self-offering of YHWH to those who had no prior category for him. In the NT: God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance (Romans 2:4); the gospel goes to the ends of the earth precisely to reach those who have not asked — the prodigal son who did not know he could return home until the word reached him (Luke 15:20: while he was still a long way off, his father saw him).
2: All day long I have held out my hands to an obstinate and rebellious people, who walk in ways not good, pursuing their own imaginations. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:21 as the counterpoint to v 1: while Israel had YHWH's arms extended toward them all day, the Gentiles received what Israel forfeited. The all-day-long-outstretched-hands is the image of unrequited divine love — the same image as Luke 13:34 (how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing). The obstinate-and-rebellious people are not permanently abandoned — Romans 11 shows that their stumbling is not final (Romans 11:11-12) — but the all-day-outstretched-arms that Israel ignored are accepted by the Gentiles who were not looking.
3: A people who continually provoke me to my face, sacrificing in gardens and burning incense on altars of brick. The specific idolatry: garden-sanctuaries and brick-altars are the Canaanite practices that violated the Mosaic prohibition of unauthorized worship sites. The provoke-to-my-face is the directness of the offense: this is not ignorant worship of another god but deliberate rejection of YHWH's prescribed worship while claiming covenant identity. In the NT: they are enemies of the cross of Christ (Philippians 3:18) — the community that has seen the gospel and turned away is more guilty than those who never heard.
4: Who sit in tombs and spend their nights keeping secret vigil; who eat the flesh of pigs, and whose pots hold broth of impure meat. The three covenant violations — sitting among tombs (ritual impurity), necromantic vigils (seeking the dead for guidance), and eating pork (Levitical prohibition) — are the explicit abandonment of covenant identity. The contrast with the Servant who did not commit violence or speak deceit (Isaiah 53:9) and the community of the new covenant that keeps YHWH's ways is total. In the NT: purity is redefined from external (Levitical food laws) to internal (what comes out of a person's heart — Matthew 15:19-20) by Jesus — but the radical violation of covenant in all three dimensions here is the mark of apostasy, not Gentile ignorance.
5: Who say, Keep to yourself, don't come near me, for I am too holy for you! These people are smoke in my nostrils, a fire that keeps burning all day. The keep-away-I-am-too-holy is the supreme irony: the idolaters who sit among tombs eating pig's flesh claim to be more holy than others — the holiness-claim that is smoke-in-the-nostrils of the actual Holy One. Jesus addresses the same irony: woe to you, teachers of the law and Pharisees, you hypocrites! You are like whitewashed tombs, which look beautiful on the outside but on the inside are full of the bones of the dead (Matthew 23:27). The smoke-in-the-nostrils-all-day is the irritating persistence of false-holiness that claims to speak for YHWH while rejecting his actual presence and instruction.
6: See, it stands written before me: I will not keep silent but will repay — I will repay into their laps both your sins and the sins of your ancestors together, says YHWH. The written-before-me is the divine record that will be opened at judgment: YHWH is not improvising his response but executing a verdict already established. The repay-into-their-laps is the measurement-back-into-the-bosom image (Luke 6:38: good measure, pressed down, shaken together, running over, will be put into your lap). In the NT: the books were opened (Revelation 20:12), and the dead were judged according to what was written in the books, according to what they had done. The written-before-me of Isaiah 65:6 is the ledger opened at the great white throne.
7: Both your own sins and the sins of your ancestors together, says YHWH; because they burned incense on the mountains and defied me on the hills, I will repay them in full for their former deeds. The accumulated-sin of multiple generations: the judgment falls on the community that perpetuated the pattern of its ancestors. The mountain-idolatry is the high-place worship that the covenant forbade and that every generation of unfaithful Israel continued. In the NT: fill up the measure of the sin of your ancestors (Matthew 23:32) — Jesus addresses the same generational-sin-accumulation to the Jerusalem leadership of his day, the final generation before the judgment of 70 CE.
8: Thus says YHWH: As the new wine is found in the cluster, and they say, Do not destroy it, for there is a blessing in it, so I will do for my servants' sake — I will not destroy them all. The grape-cluster parable: the entire cluster is not discarded because some good grapes remain within it. The faithful remnant within Israel is the preserving agent that prevents total destruction. In the NT: so too, at the present time there is a remnant chosen by grace (Romans 11:5) — the remnant of Jewish believers (Paul, the apostles, the Jerusalem church) is the good-grape-cluster that preserves the continuity of the covenant people. The do-not-destroy-for-there-is-blessing is the logic of intercession: Abraham arguing for Sodom (Genesis 18:23-32), Moses for Israel (Exodus 32:11-14).
9: I will bring forth descendants from Jacob, and from Judah those who will possess my mountains; my chosen people will inherit them, and there my servants will live. The chosen-people who inherit the mountains are the faithful remnant who receive what the idolaters forfeited. The inheritance of the mountains — Zion, the holy mountain — is the land-promise fulfilled in the community of the Servant. In the NT: the meek will inherit the earth (Matthew 5:5) — the same inheritance-language applied to the community of the Beatitudes, whose poverty-of-spirit and mourning and hunger for righteousness mark them as the chosen-people-who-will-possess.
10: Sharon will become a pasture for flocks and the Valley of Achor a place for herds to lie down, for my people who seek me. The Sharon plain (fertile coast) and the Valley of Achor (where Achan's sin brought judgment, Joshua 7:26) become pastoral blessing: the place of judgment becomes the place of rest. The Hosea tradition applies this transformation: I will give her the Valley of Achor as a door of hope (Hosea 2:15) — the place of covenant violation becomes the gateway of restoration. The my-people-who-seek-me are the counterpart of the Gentiles who found YHWH without seeking (v 1): the new covenant community is both found by the unseeker and made up of seekers. Seek and you will find (Matthew 7:7) is the call to become the my-people-who-seek.
11: But as for you who forsake YHWH and forget my holy mountain, who spread a table for Fortune and fill bowls of mixed wine for Destiny. The forsakers of YHWH are those who spread the table for Fortune (Gad) and Destiny (Meni) — the gods of luck and fate. The table-for-Fortune inverts the covenant table (Isaiah 23:5: YHWH prepares a table before me in the presence of my enemies). In the NT: the table of the Lord and the table of demons are mutually exclusive (1 Corinthians 10:21) — you cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons. The fate-and-fortune table is the NT's mammon-worship: the service of money as an alternative to YHWH's provision (Matthew 6:24).
12: I will destine you for the sword, and you will all bend down for the slaughter; for I called and you did not answer; I spoke and you did not listen; you did evil in my sight and chose what I did not desire. The called-and-did-not-answer echoes the covenant-complaint pattern of Isaiah 50:2 (when I came, was there no one there? When I called, was there no one to answer?). The choose-what-I-did-not-desire is the free-will aspect of judgment: the idolaters could have chosen YHWH's desire but chose otherwise. In the NT: you did not recognize the time of God's coming to you (Luke 19:44) — Jesus weeps over Jerusalem for the same called-and-did-not-answer failure. The destined-for-the-sword is the consequence, not the cause, of the refusal.
13: Therefore this is what the Lord YHWH says: My servants will eat, but you will go hungry; my servants will drink, but you will go thirsty; my servants will rejoice, but you will be put to shame. The servants-vs.-forsakers contrast is expressed as a feast-and-famine reversal: everything the forsakers expected from their Fortune-tables (v 11) will be denied them, while YHWH's servants receive the full covenant feast. In the NT: Jesus uses the same feast-reversal language: many who are first will be last, and many who are last will be first (Matthew 19:30). The Beatitudes are the servants' side of the reversal: blessed are the hungry, for they will be satisfied (Matthew 5:6); blessed are the mourning, for they will be comforted (Matthew 5:4).
14: My servants will sing from the joy of their heart, but you will cry from the anguish of your heart and wail from a crushed spirit. The singing-from-joy vs. crying-from-anguish is the ultimate reversal: the two communities end in diametrically opposite emotional states. The crushed spirit of the forsakers inverts the crushed spirit of the contrite that YHWH dwells with (Isaiah 57:15) — the contrite who came to YHWH receive the revived spirit; those who forsook YHWH end with the spirit crushed by judgment. In the NT: there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth (Matthew 8:12, Isaiah 22:13, Isaiah 25:30) — the anguish of those outside the feast is the forsakers' wailing of Isaiah 65:14.
15: You will leave your name as a curse to my chosen ones, and the Lord YHWH will put you to death; but to his servants he will give another name. The forsakers' name becomes a curse-formula (as bad as so-and-so) while the servants receive a new name — the reversal of identity. In the NT: the new name given to the servants (Revelation 2:17: I will give him a white stone with a new name written on it; Revelation 3:12: I will write on him the name of my God and my own new name) is the fulfillment of the other-name promise here. The servants who bear the name of Christ (Christian — Acts 11:26) carry the new-name-of-the-Servant as their identity, replacing whatever names they bore before.
16: Whoever invokes a blessing in the land will do so by the God of truth (Elohim amen); whoever takes an oath in the land will swear by the God of truth. For the former troubles are forgotten and hidden from my eyes. The God of truth (literally, God of Amen) is the divine self-designation as the reliable witness — the one whose words are always true. In the NT: Jesus uniquely introduces his teaching with Truly (Amen) I say to you — the Amen-formula is Jesus speaking with the authority of the God of Amen himself. John's Gospel uses I am the Truth (John 14:6) — Jesus is not only the truth-teller but the truth-embodied. The former-troubles-forgotten is the same promise as Isaiah 43:18 (do not remember the former things) — the new creation makes the old grief irrelevant.
17: See, I will create new heavens and a new earth. The former things will not be remembered, nor will they come to mind. The new-creation oracle is the theological apex of Isaiah and one of the most directly NT-cited verses in the OT. Peter uses it in 2 Peter 3:13 (in keeping with his promise we are looking forward to a new heaven and a new earth, where righteousness dwells). John sees it in Revelation: then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had passed away (Revelation 21:1). Paul frames the transition theologically: the present form of this world is passing away (1 Corinthians 7:31); if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come (2 Corinthians 5:17). The new-heavens-and-new-earth of Isaiah 65:17 is both the goal of history and the already-present reality inaugurated in the resurrection.
18: But be glad and rejoice forever in what I will create, for I will create Jerusalem to be a delight and its people a joy. The Jerusalem-of-delight is the new covenant community's identity: the city that was called Forsaken (Isaiah 62:4) and was lamented as a widow (Lamentations) is now created-to-be-delight by YHWH's own creative act. In the NT: the new Jerusalem coming down from God out of heaven, prepared as a bride beautifully dressed for her husband (Revelation 21:2) is the final form of the Jerusalem-of-delight. The be-glad-and-rejoice-forever is the eschatological form of the Beatitudes' final promise: rejoice and be glad, for great is your reward in heaven (Matthew 5:12).
19: I will rejoice over Jerusalem and take delight in my people; the sound of weeping and crying will be heard in it no more. The divine rejoicing over Jerusalem is the Zephaniah 3:17 promise (the Lord your God is with you; he is mighty to save; he will take great delight in you; he will rejoice over you with singing) applied eschatologically. In the NT: Revelation 21:4 is the direct fulfillment — he will wipe every tear from their eyes; there will be no more death, mourning, crying, or pain, for the old order has passed away. The no-more-sound-of-weeping is the new-creation condition where the mourning of the covenant community finally ends — the promise that frames the entire Beatitudes of Matthew 5:3-12.
20: Never again will there be in it an infant who lives but a few days, or an old man who does not live out his years; the one who dies at a hundred will be thought a mere child; the one who fails to reach a hundred will be considered accursed. The extension of life in the new creation is the reversal of the curse of Genesis 3:17-19: the shortening of life by sin's consequence is undone by YHWH's creative renewal. The hundred-year-old as a mere child signals the eschatological lifespan — not just long life but the full realization of what life was designed to be. In the NT: I am the resurrection and the life; whoever believes in me will live, even though they die (John 11:25); death is swallowed up in victory (1 Corinthians 15:54) — the eschatological extension of life becomes the eternal life of the resurrection.
21: They will build houses and dwell in them; they will plant vineyards and eat their fruit. The reversal of the curse of Deuteronomy 28:30 (you will build a house but not live in it; you will plant a vineyard but not enjoy its fruit) — the new creation is the uncursing of the covenant community's existence. The simple enjoyment of one's own labor — building and living, planting and eating — is the Eden-restoration that sin's curse interrupted. In the NT, the vision of the new creation where the city is inhabited, its gates are never shut (Revelation 21:25), and the tree of life bears fruit for healing (Revelation 22:2) is the full realization of Isaiah 65:21.
22: No longer will they build for others to inhabit, nor plant for others to eat; for as the days of a tree, so will be the days of my people; my chosen ones will long enjoy the work of their hands. The days-of-a-tree longevity — the cedar of Lebanon that spans centuries — is the new covenant community's lifespan. The work-of-their-hands that they long-enjoy is the reversal of futility: the covenant curse of labor-in-vain (the sweat of the face that yields nothing lasting, Genesis 3:19) is replaced by durable fruitfulness. In the NT: your labor in the Lord is not in vain (1 Corinthians 15:58) — because the resurrection has validated every act done in covenant faithfulness, nothing done for the Lord will ultimately be lost or taken.
23: They will not labor in vain or bear children doomed to misfortune; for they will be a people blessed by YHWH, they and their descendants with them. The blessed-by-YHWH people who do not labor in vain are the fulfillment of the Beatitudes' promise: blessed are the poor in spirit, the mourning, the meek — for theirs is the kingdom, they will be comforted, they will inherit the earth (Matthew 5:3-5). The children-not-doomed-to-misfortune is the anti-Molechian promise: where child-sacrifice sacrificed children to false gods, YHWH's new creation protects children under covenant blessing. The they-and-their-descendants blessing is the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 17:7: I will establish my covenant... as an everlasting covenant... to be your God and the God of your descendants) applied to the restored Israel.
24: Before they call I will answer; while they are still speaking I will hear. The before-they-call-I-answer is the supreme expression of anticipatory grace: YHWH's response precedes the request. The prayer-answered-before-it-is-prayed is the eschatological form of the covenant promise. In the NT: your Father knows what you need before you ask him (Matthew 6:8) — Jesus teaches the Lord's Prayer in the context of a God who already knows. The most striking instance: at Gethsemane, before the prayer is complete, the angel comes to strengthen him (Luke 22:43). The before-they-call-I-answer is the pattern of the God who sent Christ while we were still sinners (Romans 5:8) — the answer sent before the prayer was properly formed.
25: The wolf and the lamb will feed together, and the lion will eat straw like the ox, and dust will be the serpent's food. They will neither harm nor destroy on all my holy mountain, says YHWH. The new-creation peace of the animal kingdom echoes the paradise-restored oracle of Isaiah 11:6-9 (the wolf will live with the lamb; the leopard will lie down with the goat) and returns the creation to the Edenic harmony before the fall. The dust-is-the-serpent's-food is the reversal of Genesis 3:14 (and you will eat dust all the days of your life): the serpent's judgment continues — it is still reduced to dust-eating — but it can no longer harm on YHWH's holy mountain. In the NT: the serpent of old, who is the devil and Satan (Revelation 12:9), is bound for a thousand years (Revelation 20:2-3) and finally thrown into the lake of fire (Revelation 20:10) — the complete resolution of the serpent's curse that neither-harms-nor-destroys becomes permanent. The holy mountain where no harm comes is the New Jerusalem where nothing impure will ever enter (Revelation 21:27).
